title: A Terrain-Adaptive epsilon-Constraint MPC for Uneven Terrain Kinodynamic Planning
abstract: 在崎岖地形上对类车机器人进行运动动力学规划需要同时优化路径效率与姿态稳定性等多重竞争目标。本研究提出一种自适应epsilon约束法并将其集成至模型预测控制框架中，该方法的epsilon边界根据地形描述符动态调整，从而实时探索帕累托前沿。为捕捉车辆-地形交互动力学特性，我们开发了一种半参数模型，该模型将解析车辆动力学与基于相同地形描述符训练的稀疏高斯过程相结合。所提出的epsilon-MPC方法与MPPI及GAKD基线进行对比评估，实现了94%的导航成功率，同时将最大方向偏差降低了24%，并将多目标权衡质量提升了23%。

-Constraint MPC for Uneven Terrain Kinodynamic Planning
Otobong Jerome

Abstract
Kinodynamic planning for car-like vehicles on uneven terrain requires simultaneously optimizing competing objectives such as path efficiency and pose stability. This work presents an adaptive
ϵ
\epsilon
-constraint method integrated into a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, where the
ϵ
\epsilon
bounds are dynamically
adjusted based on terrain descriptors to explore the Pareto front in real time. To capture vehicle-terrain dynamics, we develop a semi-parametric model combining
analytical vehicle dynamics with a Sparse Gaussian Process (SGP) trained on the same terrain descriptors. The proposed
ϵ
\epsilon
-MPC is evaluated against MPPI and GAKD baselines, achieving a
94
%
94\%
navigation success rate while reducing maximum orientation deviation by
24
%
24\%
and improving multi-objective trade-off quality by
23

23\%
.
I
Introduction
Various methods have been proposed for generating triangular meshes from sensor data
[
29
,
30
]
, including applications in large-scale environments. Robotics frameworks such as Move Base Flex (MBF)
[
19
]
offer adaptable tools for creating so-called Navigation Meshes and enabling robot navigation within these environments. However, navigating through complex terrains still poses significant challenges for autonomous robots, especially in unstructured environments such as outdoor construction sites, disaster zones, or planetary exploration. Robust navigation in these settings requires planners that not only compute collision-free paths but also respect the kinodynamic constraints imposed by the robot’s dynamics, including velocity, acceleration limits however accurate vehicle-terrain modelling is challenging due to the effects of the terrain.
Traditional mesh planners, such as the continuous vector planner (CVP)
[
20
]
, effectively leverage the Fast Marching Method (FMM)
[
10
]
on multilayer 3D triangular meshes to generate smooth geodesic paths over rough terrains. These planners provide a global, continuous shortest-path vector field that encapsulates detailed terrain attributes such as roughness and elevation. Despite their success in producing terrain-aware geometric paths, they do not explicitly incorporate kinodynamic constraints, limiting their applicability to scenarios where dynamic feasibility is critical.
Kinodynamic planning addresses motion planning problems under both kinematic and dynamic constraints.
The first polynomial-time, provably good approximation to the solution was presented in
[
2
]
, along with a corresponding complexity analysis. Earlier work also explored solutions using randomized approaches, variants of RRT and RRT⁢, which incorporate kinodynamic cost optimization
[
12
,
4
]
. Recent kinodynamic planning research falls into two main categories: non-data-driven and data-driven approaches. Gradient-free methods like Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control are favoured in the former for managing complex dynamics without explicit gradients. Data-driven methods use models such as Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to learn system dynamics and optimize planning in high-dimensional or partially observed settings.
Recent advances in DRL have demonstrated potential for learning complex navigation policies in dynamic environments
[
9
,
13
]
, yet several critical limitations persist. Most existing approaches are confined to 2D planning and sparse reward functions, with poor generalization across diverse start-goal configurations. Furthermore, current DRL-based planners lack integration with continuous global guidance structures and do not account for kinodynamic constraints, particularly on uneven terrain. Standard RL reward formulations also fail to capture the multi-objective nature of kinodynamic planning, which can be solved using an
ϵ
\epsilon
-constraint methods, where path optimality and physical stability must be jointly considered. Although,
ϵ
\epsilon
-constraint methods have been extensively applied in multi-objective optimization research, their use in uneven terrain kinodynamic planning remains under-explored in the existing literature. Hence the contributions of this work are:
•
A semi-parametric model of car-like vehicle motion on uneven terrain represented as a triangular mesh.
•
A cost function for mesh navigation based on a continuous vector field.
•
A solution approach based on terrain-adaptive epsilon-constraint optimization within a receding horizon control framework for generating non-dominated trajectories.
•
A Robot Operating System (ROS) planner compatible with Move Base Flex (MBF)
[
19
]
.
II
Related Work

Kinodynamic planning on 3D triangular meshes draws heavily from geodesic computation on triangular meshes. Classic discrete geodesic methods include the MMP
[
16
]
and CH
[
3
]
algorithms (and their variants
[
32
,
21
]
), known for precision on smaller meshes, alongside the fast marching method (FMM)
[
10
]
and heat method
[
5
]
, which scale better to larger datasets. The more recent edge-flip method
[
25
]
refines geodesic paths via local mesh modifications, though at higher computational cost. Comprehensive surveys of these approaches are provided in
[
1
,
8
]
. Kinodynamic planning on such meshes then requires balancing proximity to the optimal geodesic against the robot’s kinematic and dynamic constraints.
Gradient-based kinodynamic planning methods including Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP)
[
6
]
, Model Predictive Control (MPC)
[
18
]
, Covariant Hamiltonian Optimization for Motion Planning (CHOMP)
[
35
,
22
]
, and Iterative Linear Quadratic Regulator (ILQR)
[
27
]
offer strong convergence properties but face limitations such as sensitivity to initial conditions, local optima trapping, and high online computation costs, particularly in non-linear and non-convex settings. Gradient-free alternatives like Model Predictive Path Integral Control (MPPI)
[
31
]
and Genetic Algorithmic Kinodynamic (GAKD) planning
[
7
]
circumvent these issues by avoiding gradient computations entirely: MPPI evaluates multiple independent control sequences in parallel for broad solution space coverage, while GAKD extends this by replacing cost-weighted trajectory averaging with a genetic algorithm that selects and recombines the best trajectory segments
[
7
]
, enabling robust real-time performance in dynamically challenging environments. However, these methods rely on accurate dynamics models that can be difficult to derive analytically for car-like vehicles on arbitrary 3D mesh terrain; to this end, one contributions of this paper is to provide a semi-parametric approach blending data-driven flexibility with physical structure. Moreover, in MPPI and GAKD, weighted scalarization typically allows the path length objective to dominate the solution, so we propose a cost formulation that prevents this dominance and yields a balanced multi-objective outcome.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) has shown promise for mobile robot navigation but faces challenges including sparse rewards, slow convergence, poor generalizability, and limited interpretability. Various hybrid strategies address these limitations, hierarchical architectures combining global planners with local RL agents
[
28
,
33
]
, sensor-based feedback and transfer learning
[
34
,
11
]
, modular designs decoupling deep learning components
[
24
,
26
]
, and attention mechanisms for improved interpretability
[
9
]
, with a comprehensive review available in
[
23
]
. However, these approaches are predominantly designed for 2D planar environments or rely on elevation maps that do not fully capture the intricacies of uneven 3D mesh terrain.
Hence, there is a need for a planner that explicitly addresses the inherent multi-objective nature of kinodynamic planning, balancing path optimality against physical stability, while generalizing to unseen uneven terrain configurations.
The
ϵ
\epsilon
-constraint method is a classical technique in multi-objective optimization that converts a multi-objective problem into a series of single-objective sub-problems by constraining all but one objective within specified bounds. For instance,
[
17
]
and
[
14
]
explored the use of the
ϵ
\epsilon
-constraint method in intelligent parking lot power management and engineering optimization problems. While effective at generating Pareto-optimal solutions, the method’s computational cost limits its practicality in high-dimensional or real-time settings.
In this work, this limitations is circumvented by sampling from the Pareto front, and applying the method within a receding horizon, also, the stability constraint is adaptively tightened or relaxed based on the local terrain conditions. A scaled continuous vector field is leveraged to provide global guidance, enabling consistent performance across diverse mesh environments. The proposed terrain-adaptive epsilon-constraint method systematically explore the trade-off frontier between competing objectives. The proposed semi-parametric vehicle-terrain dynamics model, enables offline development and generalization for online deployment.
III
Methodology
The uneven terrain planning is formulated as a receding horizon kinodynamic problem, seeking dynamically feasible trajectories that optimize multiple objectives while using the FMM vector field for global guidance.
The problem is solved as an epsilon-constraint optimization: at each planning iteration, forward trajectories are simulated, and the sequence minimizing a progress objective is selected subject to a hard constraint whose tolerance is dynamically adjusted to local terrain characteristics. Candidate sequences are produced through warm-starting and Fast Marching Method (FMM)-biased sampling, by which exploration is guided toward promising regions of the environment. Along each trajectory, terrain descriptors are extracted and orientation deviations from a terrain-aligned reference frame are computed, yielding a physically meaningful stability risk measure. The resulting receding-horizon controller adapts both its constraint threshold and sampling strategy to the terrain being traversed, ensuring less computation on benign terrain while conservative behaviour is automatically enforced in challenging conditions.

The terrain-adaptive pose stability formulation inherently adapts to local geometric conditions: orientations that would be unstable on flat terrain become necessary on moderate slopes, while excessively steep regions are marked as lethal obstacles by the FMM computation, generating vector fields that point the vehicle away. The terrain-adaptive epsilon constraint further enhances this adaptability by automatically tightening stability requirements in challenging terrain while allowing more aggressive exploration in benign conditions.
V
Evaluation
We evaluate the proposed terrain-adaptive kinodynamic planning approach through comprehensive simulation experiments designed to assess navigation performance across diverse terrain conditions. The proposed evaluation focuses on three key research questions: (1) Does the FMM-biased sampling strategy improve trajectory quality compared to pure random sampling? (2) How does the terrain-adaptive epsilon constraint affect stability and path efficiency across varying terrain difficulties? (3) Can the planner handle dynamic receding horizon replanning in real-time while maintaining safety?

V-E
Real-World Experiments
To validate the simulation findings, we deployed the proposed planner on a physical AgileX Scout Mini robot in an outdoor environment.
The Scout Mini was equipped with a Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR for terrain perception, and connected to an ASUS ROG Strix G614JU mobile workstation equipped with a 13th Gen Intel Core i7 processor, 32 GB DDR5 RAM (5600 MT/s), NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4050 GPU, and a 512 GB NVMe SSD, running Ubuntu Linux.
The real-world result is close to simulation trends: success rates due to unmodeled physical effects like wheel slip and sensor noise, while path deviations increase 22% as the planner selects more conservative routes under uncertainty. Real-world planning times are 15% slower but still meet the 100 ms real-time requirement. The proposed method’s performance advantages over baselines persist in physical deployment, validating that terrain-adaptive constraints scale appropriately to real-world conditions.

The proposed method achieves 80% success rate compared to MPPI’s 67% and GAKD’s 73%. The stability improvements are particularly pronounced, with the proposed method limiting orientation deviation to 26.4° compared to GAKD’s 30.2° and MPPI’s 32.8°, maintaining a safe margin from the vehicle’s 35° mechanical tip-over threshold. MPPI’s failures resulted from excessive roll angles on steep terrain sections, while the proposed terrain-adaptive approach successfully navigated these same regions by tightening stability constraints based on local slope measurements.
V-F
Failure Analysis
Failed trials exhibited three modes: infeasible scenarios where goals lie in FMM-unreachable regions, local minima trapping in narrow corridors (mitigable by increasing
N
cand
N_{\text{cand}}
to 100), and stability constraint violations at terrain discontinuities where descriptor extraction incompletely captures local geometry. We also explored NSGA-II multi-objective optimization to jointly optimize path efficiency and stability, but its population-based evolutionary search incurred planning times exceeding 2000 ms per iteration, over an order of magnitude slower than MPPI (29 ms) and GAKD (35 ms), while yielding comparable or worse success rates, confirming that for real-time receding horizon planning, simpler sampling-based methods with direct FMM guidance offer superior efficiency without sacrificing solution quality.
V-G
Conclusion and Future Work
The results demonstrate that while weighted scalarization methods can optimize individual objectives effectively, the proposed terrain-adaptive
ϵ
\epsilon
-constraint approach more effectively balances competing objectives by dynamically adjusting stability constraints based on local terrain features, enabling safer navigation without excessive path deviation. The modest computational overhead is justified by substantial improvements in feasibility and completion rate, particularly critical for autonomous systems in unstructured outdoor environments.
The presented approach provides a principled framework for terrain-adaptive kinodynamic planning that balances global optimality, local safety, and computational tractability, with several promising directions for future work. Extending the framework to dynamic environments represents a natural next step, where integrating temporal prediction into FMM computation and stability assessment would enable robust navigation alongside moving obstacles. Finally, expanding the terrain descriptor with additional semantic properties such as surface softness offers an exciting opportunity to refine traversability assessment and broaden the range of deployable environments.

Kinodynamic planning for car-like vehicles on uneven terrain requires simultaneously optimizing competing objectives such as path efficiency and pose stability. This work presents an adaptive epsilon-constraint method integrated into a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, where the epsilon bounds are dynamically adjusted based on terrain descriptors to explore the Pareto front in real time. To capture vehicle-terrain dynamics, we develop a semi-parametric model combining analytical vehicle dynamics with a Sparse Gaussian Process (SGP) trained on the same terrain descriptors. The proposed epsilon-MPC is evaluated against MPPI and GAKD baselines, achieving a 94% navigation success rate while reducing maximum orientation deviation by 24% and improving multi-objective trade-off quality by 23%.
